g.data-narration: Welcome to Propositional versus First-Order Inference. This module explores two fundamental logical frameworks for AI reasoning.
Inference is deriving new facts from known facts using logical reasoning. It's the core of AI systems that learn and deduce.
Propositional Logic uses atomic propositions that are either true or false, combined with logical connectives like AND, OR, NOT.
Modus Ponens is the fundamental inference rule: from P and P implies Q, we infer Q. This works in both propositional and first-order logic.
g.text: Modus Ponens: The Basic Inference Rule

g.data-narration: Propositional logic has a critical flaw: it cannot represent general rules about categories. Each human requires a separate proposition.
g.text: Socrates is human → Socrates is mortal
Plato is human → Plato is mortal
Aristotle is human → Aristotle is mortal
...and we need infinitely many more for each human!

g.data-narration: First-Order Logic solves this by using variables, predicates, and quantifiers. One rule covers ALL humans using universal quantifier.
g.text: "For all x, if x is human,

g.data-narration: FOL has four key components: objects (entities), predicates (properties/relations), variables (placeholders), and quantifiers (∀ and ∃).
Universal Instantiation derives P(c) from ∀x P(x) for any constant c. Existential Instantiation creates a new constant for ∃x P(x).
g.text: ∀x P(x) ⊢ P(c) for any constant c
∃x P(x) ⊢ P(c) for NEW constant c

g.data-narration: Unification finds substitutions that make two atomic formulas identical. It's fundamental to FOL inference and requires the occurs check.
g.text: Process of finding substitutions θ such that P·θ = Q·θ

g.data-narration: The unification algorithm checks if expressions are identical, applies substitutions to variables, performs occurs check, and recurses on compound expressions.
Let's unify P(x, f(y)) with P(g(z), f(a)). We substitute x with g(z), and y with a to make them identical.
Resolution is a complete inference rule for FOL. It derives new clauses from two parent clauses by canceling complementary literals.
To prove Socrates is mortal: convert to clauses, negate goal, resolve until empty clause appears. Empty means contradiction - proof complete.
Forward chaining starts from facts and applies rules forward. Backward chaining starts from goal and works backwards to find supporting facts.
g.text: Start with known, apply rules forward

g.data-narration: Propositional inference is NP-complete - exponentially hard. FOL is semi-decidable - no guarantee of finding proof in finite time.
Key differences: FOL uses variables and quantifiers for general rules, while propositional requires explicit facts for each instance.
g.text: Key Takeaway: FOL solves the representation problem

g.data-narration: Master Modus Ponens, instantiation rules, unification, and resolution. Remember: FOL enables general rules about categories using quantifiers.
g.text: Exam Tips: Master these inference rules
Modus Ponens | Universal/Existential Instantiation | Unification | Resolution
